moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the English Opening, a flexible system that controls the center from the side rather than occupying it directly with pawns. By playing c4, we stake a claim on d5 and prepare to support e4 later if needed, while keeping our options open for various pawn structures. This move has been a favorite of world champions like Capablanca and Kasparov because it avoids the theoretical battles of 1.e4 and 1.d4 while maintaining winning chances. The c-file will likely become important for our rooks as the game develops.

Black opts for a fianchetto setup, signaling an intention to develop the bishop to g7 where it will control the long diagonal and pressure our center from afar. This move makes the Great Snake Variation unique—Black prioritizes flexibility and piece activity over central pressure, which appeals to players who want to avoid heavy theory. The move is solid and popularized by players like Kasparov and Karpov, preparing a kingside fianchetto while keeping the center fluid. Black's idea is to let us define the center while their pieces become active and well-coordinated.
